subject: Advanced Java Programming
description: Comprehensive Java learning resource with key concepts, common errors, learning tips, and examples for all topics.

JV-BAS-1.1.name: Java Installation and Environment Setup
JV-BAS-1.1.summary: Setting up a Java development environment requires installing the JDK, configuring PATH, and choosing an IDE. Understanding the distinction between JDK, JRE, and JVM is essential.
keyConcepts: JDK (Java Development Kit): Full toolkit including compiler (javac), JRE, and development tools
JRE (Java Runtime Environment): Minimum needed to run Java programs
JVM (Java Virtual Machine): Interprets bytecode; provides platform independence
IntelliJ IDEA: Most popular Java IDE (Community edition free)
Eclipse: Free, open-source IDE with vast plugin ecosystem
VS Code: Lightweight editor with Java Extension Pack
Maven: Dependency management and build automation tool
Gradle: Flexible build tool using Groovy or Kotlin DSL
JAVA_HOME: Environment variable pointing to JDK installation directory
LTS Releases: Long-Term Support versions (Java 8, 11, 17, 21) recommended for production
commonErrors: Confusing JDK and JRE — always install JDK for development
Not setting JAVA_HOME environment variable
Having multiple JDK versions causing version conflicts
Forgetting to add JAVA_HOME/bin to PATH
Using an outdated JDK version incompatible with libraries
Running javac when Java is installed but JDK is not
learningTips: Use SDKMAN! (Linux/Mac) or Chocolatey (Windows) to manage multiple JDK versions
Verify installation with 'java -version' and 'javac -version'
Start with IntelliJ IDEA Community — it has the best Java support for free
Always prefer LTS versions for learning and production
Use Maven or Gradle from day one — avoid manual classpath management
socraticPrompts: Why is Java called 'write once, run anywhere'? What role does the JVM play?
What's the difference between compilation in Java vs Python?
Why would you choose Gradle over Maven for a new project?
What happens if you have Java 11 installed but your project requires Java 17?
Why should you use LTS versions of Java in production?

codeExample.description: Verifying Java installation and basic compilation
codeExample.output:
java version "21.0.1" 2023-10-17 LTS
Java(TM) SE Runtime Environment (build 21.0.1+12-LTS)
Java HotSpot(TM) 64-Bit Server VM (build 21.0.1+12-LTS, mixed mode)
javac 21.0.1

JV-BAS-1.2.name: Your First Java Program
JV-BAS-1.2.summary: Every Java program starts with a class definition containing a main method. Understanding the structure of a Java source file — class, method, statement — is the foundation of Java programming.
keyConcepts: Class definition: Java code must be inside a class
public static void main(String[] args): The entry point of every Java application
System.out.println(): Prints output and adds a newline
Statements end with semicolons in Java
File name must match the public class name (HelloWorld.java → public class HelloWorld)
Compilation: javac produces .class bytecode files
Execution: java runs the bytecode on the JVM
Package declaration: Organises classes into namespaces (optional for simple programs)
commonErrors: File name not matching class name (case-sensitive)
Forgetting semicolons at end of statements
Misspelling 'public static void main(String[] args)'
Trying to run .java file directly instead of compiling first
Missing opening or closing braces {}
Putting code outside a method (only field declarations allowed at class level)
learningTips: Type the program out rather than copying — muscle memory matters
Read compiler error messages carefully — they tell you the exact line
Use an IDE to catch syntax errors before compilation
Understand each word in 'public static void main(String[] args)' — each has meaning
Practice running from the terminal as well as the IDE
socraticPrompts: Why must the file name match the public class name in Java?
What does 'static' mean in 'public static void main'? Why is main static?
What's the difference between System.out.print() and System.out.println()?
What happens to the .class file after you run the program?
Why does Java require a class even for the simplest program?

codeExample.description: Structure of a complete Java program
codeExample.output:
Hello, World!
Java is awesome!
Welcome to Java 21!

JV-OOP-1.1.name: Class Definition and Object Creation
JV-OOP-1.1.summary: Classes are blueprints for objects. An object is an instance of a class, allocated on the heap. Understanding the relationship between a class definition and object creation is fundamental to Java OOP.
keyConcepts: Class: A blueprint defining fields (state) and methods (behaviour)
Object: A runtime instance of a class, created with 'new'
Heap: Where objects are stored in memory
Reference variable: Stores the memory address of an object
null: Default value for reference variables — means 'no object'
new keyword: Allocates memory and calls the constructor
Garbage Collection: JVM automatically reclaims unreferenced objects
Multiple references: Several variables can refer to the same object
commonErrors: NullPointerException: Calling a method on a null reference
Confusing the class (blueprint) with the object (instance)
Forgetting 'new' when creating an object
Expecting '==' to compare object content — it compares references
Not understanding that assignments copy references, not objects
learningTips: Draw memory diagrams showing heap objects and stack references
Always check for null before dereferencing a reference
Use equals() to compare object content, == for primitives and references identity
Think of a class as a cookie cutter and objects as the cookies
socraticPrompts: What happens in memory when you write 'Dog d1 = new Dog()'?
If Dog d2 = d1, and you call d2.setName('Rex'), what is d1.getName()?
Why does Java use garbage collection instead of manual memory management?
What is the difference between a class variable and an instance variable?
When would you get a NullPointerException and how do you prevent it?

codeExample.description: Defining a class and creating objects
codeExample.output:
Buddy says: Woof!
Max says: Woof!
Buddy (Labrador, age 3)
Rocky

JV-OOP-2.3.name: Interfaces
JV-OOP-2.3.summary: Interfaces define contracts — a set of methods that implementing classes must provide. Since Java 8, interfaces can have default and static methods. Interfaces enable multiple inheritance of type in Java.
keyConcepts: interface: Declares abstract methods (implicitly public abstract)
implements: A class signs a contract to implement an interface
Multiple interfaces: A class can implement many interfaces (unlike extends)
default method (Java 8+): Provides a default implementation in the interface
static method (Java 8+): Utility method belonging to the interface itself
private method (Java 9+): Helper method inside the interface
Functional interface: Interface with exactly one abstract method (used with lambdas)
@FunctionalInterface: Annotation to enforce functional interface contract
Marker interface: Interface with no methods (e.g., Serializable, Cloneable)
commonErrors: Forgetting to implement all abstract methods of an interface
Trying to instantiate an interface directly
Confusing abstract class with interface — use interface for pure contracts
Assuming interface fields are mutable — they are public static final
Not understanding diamond problem resolution with default methods
learningTips: Prefer interfaces over abstract classes when no shared state is needed
Use functional interfaces with lambdas for cleaner code
Program to the interface, not the implementation (List not ArrayList)
Use default methods carefully — they can break existing implementations
socraticPrompts: Why can a class implement multiple interfaces but only extend one class?
When would you use an abstract class instead of an interface?
What problem do default methods solve that didn't exist before Java 8?
Why is Comparator a functional interface? How does that relate to lambdas?
What is the diamond problem and how does Java resolve it with default methods?

codeExample.description: Defining and implementing interfaces with default methods

JV-COL-1.2.name: List Implementations
JV-COL-1.2.summary: ArrayList and LinkedList are the two most common List implementations. ArrayList provides O(1) random access backed by an array; LinkedList provides O(1) head/tail operations backed by a doubly-linked list.
keyConcepts: ArrayList: Backed by a resizable array; O(1) get/set, O(n) insert/delete in middle
LinkedList: Doubly-linked list; O(1) add/remove at head/tail, O(n) random access
List<E> interface: Ordered, allows duplicates, index-based access
add(), remove(), get(), set(), size(), contains(), indexOf()
Autoboxing: Primitives auto-converted to wrapper types (int → Integer)
List.of() (Java 9+): Creates an immutable list
ArrayList capacity: Grows by 50% when full — consider initial capacity for performance
Iteration: for-each, iterator, or ListIterator (supports bidirectional traversal)
commonErrors: Using ArrayList for frequent insertions/deletions in the middle (use LinkedList)
Removing elements by index vs by object: remove(0) vs remove(Integer.valueOf(0))
ConcurrentModificationException: Modifying a list while iterating with for-each
Using == instead of equals() to check if list contains an object
Not declaring as List<E> — coding to ArrayList<E> prevents easy switching
learningTips: Default to ArrayList — it outperforms LinkedList in most real-world scenarios
Use iterator.remove() if you need to remove while iterating
Declare as List<E> (interface type) to keep code flexible
Use Collections.unmodifiableList() or List.of() for read-only lists
Pre-size ArrayList if you know the approximate number of elements
socraticPrompts: When would you choose LinkedList over ArrayList?
Why does removing by index work differently from removing by object value?
What is autoboxing? What are its performance implications?
How would you safely remove all even numbers from an ArrayList while iterating?
What is the time complexity of ArrayList.contains() and why?

codeExample.description: ArrayList and LinkedList usage with common operations
codeExample.output:
[Avocado, Apple, Banana, Cherry]
Banana
4
[Banana, Cherry]
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
First

JV-STR-2.2.name: Intermediate Stream Operations
JV-STR-2.2.summary: Intermediate operations transform a stream into another stream. They are lazy — they are not executed until a terminal operation is invoked. Chaining intermediate operations builds a pipeline.
keyConcepts: filter(Predicate): Retains elements matching the predicate
map(Function): Transforms each element to another type
flatMap(Function): Maps each element to a stream, then flattens into one stream
distinct(): Removes duplicate elements using equals()
sorted(): Natural ordering or Comparator-based sorting
peek(Consumer): Inspects elements without consuming them (debugging)
limit(n): Truncates stream to at most n elements
skip(n): Discards first n elements
mapToInt/Long/Double: Produces primitive specialised streams for efficiency
commonErrors: Reusing a stream — streams can only be consumed once
Expecting filter() to modify the original collection — streams are non-mutating
Forgetting that intermediate ops are lazy — no work happens without a terminal op
Using peek() in production code — it's for debugging only
Confusing map() (1-to-1) with flatMap() (1-to-many flattened)
learningTips: Think of a stream pipeline as an assembly line — each station transforms elements
Use peek() liberally during development to debug pipeline behaviour
Prefer method references (String::toUpperCase) over lambdas when possible
Order matters for performance: filter early to reduce elements for later ops
Use primitive streams (IntStream) for numeric ops to avoid boxing overhead
socraticPrompts: Why are stream operations lazy? What performance benefit does this give?
What's the difference between map() and flatMap()? Give a concrete example.
Why can't you reuse a stream after a terminal operation?
How would you debug a complex stream pipeline?
When would sorted() after filter() be more efficient than the reverse?

JV-CON-2.1.name: Executor Framework
JV-CON-2.1.summary: The Executor framework decouples task submission from task execution. ExecutorService manages a pool of threads, reusing them to avoid the overhead of creating new threads per task.
keyConcepts: ExecutorService: Interface for managing and controlling thread execution
Executors factory: Creates common pool types (Fixed, Cached, Single, Scheduled)
newFixedThreadPool(n): Pool with n threads — best for CPU-bound tasks
newCachedThreadPool(): Grows as needed — best for short-lived I/O tasks
newSingleThreadExecutor(): Guarantees sequential task execution
submit(): Submits Runnable or Callable, returns Future
shutdown(): Initiates orderly shutdown — no new tasks accepted
shutdownNow(): Attempts to stop all running tasks
awaitTermination(): Waits for completion up to a timeout
Future.get(): Blocks until result available; throws ExecutionException on failure
commonErrors: Forgetting to shutdown the ExecutorService — causes the JVM to hang
Calling Future.get() on the submission thread — blocks calling thread
Using newCachedThreadPool() for CPU-bound tasks — creates too many threads
Not handling ExecutionException from Future.get()
Submitting tasks after shutdown — causes RejectedExecutionException
learningTips: Always shut down ExecutorService — use try-finally or try-with-resources pattern
Use CompletableFuture for non-blocking async composition (Java 8+)
Rule of thumb: FixedThreadPool(Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()) for CPU tasks
Use ScheduledExecutorService for recurring tasks instead of Timer
Prefer Callable over Runnable when you need a return value or exception propagation
socraticPrompts: Why is creating a new Thread for every task inefficient?
What happens if all threads in a FixedThreadPool are busy and you submit another task?
Why do you need to call shutdown() on an ExecutorService?
What is the difference between submit() and execute()?
When would you prefer a single-thread executor over a fixed thread pool?

codeExample.description: Using ExecutorService with Callable and Future
codeExample.output:
Task 1 on pool-1-thread-1
Task 2 on pool-1-thread-2
Task 3 on pool-1-thread-3
Task 4 on pool-1-thread-1
Task 5 on pool-1-thread-2
Result: 1
Result: 4
Result: 9
Result: 16
Result: 25

JV-DSG-1.1.name: Singleton Pattern
JV-DSG-1.1.summary: The Singleton pattern ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access. It's one of the most well-known but also most misused patterns — prefer dependency injection when possible.
keyConcepts: Private constructor: Prevents direct instantiation
Static instance: Holds the single instance
Lazy initialisation: Create instance only when first needed
Eager initialisation: Create instance when class loads
Double-checked locking: Thread-safe lazy init with minimal synchronisation
volatile keyword: Prevents instruction reordering with double-checked locking
Enum Singleton: Simplest thread-safe singleton, handles serialisation
Bill Pugh Singleton: Uses static inner class for lazy thread-safe init
commonErrors: Non-thread-safe singleton — two threads creating two instances
Using synchronised getInstance() — every call pays synchronisation cost
Forgetting volatile with double-checked locking — can cause subtle bugs
Overusing Singleton — makes code hard to test (can't inject mock)
Singleton broken by reflection or serialisation
learningTips: Prefer Enum Singleton — it's concise, thread-safe, and serialisation-safe
Consider dependency injection (Spring @Bean) instead of Singleton pattern
Use for truly shared resources: configuration, logging, connection pools
Never use Singleton for mutable shared state — leads to hidden coupling
socraticPrompts: Why is a non-thread-safe Singleton dangerous in a multi-threaded app?
What problems does the volatile keyword solve in double-checked locking?
How does the enum Singleton handle serialisation automatically?
Why is Singleton considered an anti-pattern by some developers?
How would you test code that depends on a Singleton?

JV-EXC-2.1.name: try-catch-finally
JV-EXC-2.1.summary: Java uses try-catch-finally to handle exceptions gracefully. The try block contains risky code, catch blocks handle specific exceptions, and finally always executes for cleanup — even if an exception occurs.
keyConcepts: try block: Encloses code that may throw an exception
catch block: Handles a specific exception type
Multiple catch: Order matters — catch subclasses before superclasses
finally block: Always executes (even with return statements), used for cleanup
Exception class hierarchy: Throwable → Exception → Specific exceptions
getMessage(): Returns the exception detail message
printStackTrace(): Prints the full stack trace (use loggers in production)
Multi-catch (Java 7+): catch (IOException | SQLException e) combines catches
commonErrors: Catching Exception or Throwable too broadly — hides bugs
Empty catch blocks — silently swallowing exceptions
Wrong catch order: Catching parent before child causes compile error
Relying on finally for return values — behaviour is surprising
Using exception handling for normal control flow — is expensive
learningTips: Always log the exception in the catch block — never leave it empty
Catch the most specific exception type possible
Use finally for closing resources, or better: use try-with-resources
In production: use SLF4J logger.error() instead of printStackTrace()
Re-throw if you can't meaningfully handle the exception at this level
socraticPrompts: What happens if an exception is thrown inside a finally block?
Why does catching Exception broadly hide bugs?
If a try block has a return statement and finally also has return, which return wins?
When should you re-throw an exception instead of handling it?
What's the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions?

codeExample.output:
divide() finally block executed
5
Cannot divide by zero: / by zero
divide() finally block executed
-1

JV-TST-1.1.name: JUnit 5 Basics
JV-TST-1.1.summary: JUnit 5 is the standard Java testing framework. Tests are annotated methods in test classes. Assertions verify expected behaviour. Lifecycle annotations manage setup and teardown.
keyConcepts: @Test: Marks a method as a test case
@BeforeEach: Runs before each test method — setup per test
@AfterEach: Runs after each test method — cleanup per test
@BeforeAll: Runs once before all tests in the class (must be static)
@AfterAll: Runs once after all tests in the class (must be static)
Assertions.assertEquals(expected, actual): Verifies equality
Assertions.assertThrows(ExceptionType.class, executable): Verifies exception thrown
Assertions.assertAll(): Groups multiple assertions — all run even if some fail
@DisplayName: Human-readable test name
@Disabled: Skips a test — equivalent to @Ignore in JUnit 4
commonErrors: Confusing expected and actual parameters in assertEquals (expected first!)
Writing @BeforeAll or @AfterAll on non-static methods
Testing implementation details rather than behaviour
Tests depending on order of execution — each test must be independent
Not asserting anything — a test that never fails is useless
learningTips: Follow AAA (Arrange-Act-Assert) structure within each test method
Test one behaviour per test method — keep tests focused
Use @DisplayName with descriptive names: 'should return empty list when user has no orders'
Never test private methods directly — test through public API
Aim for tests that document behaviour, not just code coverage
socraticPrompts: Why should each test be independent? What problems arise if they share state?
What is the difference between @BeforeEach and @BeforeAll?
Why put the expected value first in assertEquals(expected, actual)?
What does assertAll() give you that multiple separate asserts don't?
When would you use assertThrows instead of try-catch in a test?

codeExample.description: Complete JUnit 5 test class with lifecycle annotations
codeExample.output:
Starting Calculator test suite
testAddition — PASSED
testDivisionByZero — PASSED
testMultipleAssertions — PASSED
testSquareRoot — SKIPPED
Calculator test suite complete
